ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for lemon juice as a liquid; this record is a sweetened commercial lemon juice drink made from concentrate, which is a processed variant. Closest defensible FoodOn anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A0EJL covers fruit juice drinks; used as closest defensible anchor for this sweetened lemon juice concentrate beverage. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific commercial formulation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for sweetened commercial lemon juice drink; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one glass), providing about 103 kcal and 23 g of carbohydrates, of which approximately 23 g is sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D03070016 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific commercial lemon juice drink. GI of 66 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened fruit juice drinks / lemonade (GI ~65–67, glucose reference). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 25.8) / 100 ≈ 17.0; rounded to 15.2 using 23 g net available carbohydrate per serving. Analogous estimate basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.19 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium (15 mg/100 g), per AHA 2018 guidance.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened lemon juice drink; the 9.19 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk, and fresh-squeezed lemon juice with no added sugar is a clinically preferable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.19 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes, triggering the ADA sugar-sweetened beverage hard gate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this commercial lemon juice drink entirely; its 9.19 g/100 g sugar with zero fibre causes rapid glycaemic excursions, and it must not be confused with fresh lemon juice (approximately 2.5 g sugar/100 g) which carries a substantially lower glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular event risk; this drink provides 9.19 g sugar/100 g with no protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate this sugar-sweetened lemon juice drink, as habitual SSB consumption is independently linked to elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.19 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; zero fibre and negligible vitamin C mean no anti-inflammatory benefit is conferred.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this commercial lemon juice drink; its high added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation, and fresh lemon juice providing genuine vitamin C and flavonoids is a clinically preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon Juice Drink is a commercially produced sweetened beverage made from lemon juice concentrate, water, and added sweeteners such as sugar or high-fructose corn syrup. It is classified as an ultra-processed juice drink and is distinct from fresh-squeezed or 100% pure lemon juice. The drink has a pale yellow colour, a sweet-tart flavour profile, and a thin watery texture. It is commonly consumed chilled as a refreshment or snack beverage. Nutritionally, it provides 41 kcal per 100 g, with 10.32 g carbohydrate of which 9.19 g is sugar, and negligible protein (0 g) and fat (0 g). Protein is naturally zero as this is a carbohydrate-sweetened beverage with no protein-containing ingredients. Fat is naturally zero by product formulation — lemon juice concentrate and sweeteners contribute no fat. Dietary fibre is 0 g/100 g, confirmed per HPB Singapore data. Sodium is low at 15 mg/100 g. The high sugar content (9.19 g/100 g) with zero fibre classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), which is clinically significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 66 (medium GI), and a standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 23 g of sugar. This drink should not be confused with fresh lemon juice, which contains approximately 2.5 g sugar per 100 g and provides vitamin C and flavonoids. The HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070016) is the primary nutrient source for this record.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in lemon juice drink?
Can diabetics drink lemon juice drink?
Is lemon juice drink the same as fresh lemon juice?
Is lemon juice drink good for high blood pressure?
Is lemon juice drink a sugar-sweetened beverage?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03070016, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical). Source match is exact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened fruit juice drinks, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: Zero-macro justification (g4): protein_g is naturally zero as this is a carbohydrate-sweetened beverage containing no protein-contributing ingredients (lemon juice concentrate, water, sweeteners). fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation — lemon juice concentrate and sweeteners contribute no fat. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo data. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. Negative sentinel values (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this commercial lemon juice drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cr_id D03070016, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these fields. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source and are preserved as confirmed zero. GI note: GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened fruit juice drinks / lemonade from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific commercial product was found in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (66 × 23.0) / 100 = 15.2 for a 250 g serving. This record is a generic commercial lemon juice drink and must not be confused with fresh lemon juice (HPB ID 1415, approximately 2.5 g sugar/100 g).
